1.synthesis: As the crowd presses around Jesus to hear the word of God at the Lake of Gennesaret, he sees two boats and gets into Simon's to put out from shore, teaching the crowd from the boat (vv.1–3). Afterward he tells Simon to put out into the deep and let down the nets for a catch. Simon protests that they have toiled all night and caught nothing — "but at your word I will let down the nets" (vv.4–5). They enclose a great shoal of fish, so large the net is breaking and the boats begin to sink (vv.6–7). Simon Peter falls at Jesus's knees: "Depart from me, for I am a sinful man, O Lord" (v.8). Jesus: "Do not be afraid; from now on you will be catching men" (v.10). They leave everything and follow him (v.11). Luke's call of the disciples is entirely distinct in form from Mark/Matthew's brief lakeside call, grounding the discipleship in a miraculous sign and a personal encounter with Jesus's holiness.
1.historical_context: The Lake of Gennesaret (Sea of Galilee) was a principal fishing resource for the region; professional fishermen like Simon, Andrew, James, and John would have worked through the night (night-fishing was optimal for the tilapia common in the lake). The miraculous catch of fish has a partial parallel in John 21:1–14's post-resurrection catch; some interpreters see Luke 5 as a displaced account of the same event, while most treat them as distinct. Simon's address of Jesus as "Lord" (Kyrie, v.8) in response to the miracle is a moment of recognition — not yet full christological confession, but awe before divine power. His confession "I am a sinful man" is the response of Isaiah 6:5 ("Woe is me! For I am lost, for I am a man of unclean lips") — the prophet's response to the vision of divine holiness becomes the fisherman's response to the divine presence in Jesus.
1.literary_structure: Luke's version of the call is centered on Simon Peter, with James and John as secondary figures and Andrew absent from this account. The structure is: request/protest/obedience (vv.3–5) → miracle/amazement (vv.6–9) → commission/renunciation (vv.10–11). The pattern of protest-then-obedience ("but at your word I will let down the nets," v.5) is the paradigm of faith that acts against empirical probability on the authority of Christ's word. "From now on you will be catching men" (v.10) transforms the vocational metaphor: the skill developed as fishermen (patient effort, deep waters, nets) will be redirected toward the human catch of the gospel.
1.theological_application: "Depart from me, for I am a sinful man, O Lord" (v.8). Simon's response to the miraculous catch is not primarily about the fish but about the person in his boat — the fisherman suddenly knows he is in the presence of holiness, and holiness and sin cannot coexist without crisis. This is the moment of vocation: the recognition of unworthiness precedes the commission. Isaiah was undone before the seraphic vision; Moses removed his sandals before the burning bush; Simon falls at Jesus's knees. The call to discipleship does not begin with a survey of qualifications but with the stripping away of self-sufficiency before the Lord of the catch.
1.christology: Jesus's command to put out into the deep and let down the nets — against the professional fisherman's overnight experience of empty nets — is an act of authority over the natural world consistent with his authority over sickness and demons. When the nets fill, Simon's reaction (falling at Jesus's knees and confessing sin) is the same reaction evoked in Scripture by divine theophany: the creature in the presence of the Creator. "Do not be afraid" (v.10) is the angel's greeting and the risen Lord's first word after the resurrection — Jesus speaks to Simon's fear with the language of divine reassurance. The one who fills nets full of fish will fill the world with disciples: the miraculous catch is a parable of the mission the disciples are entering.

12.synthesis: In one of the cities, a man covered with leprosy sees Jesus, falls on his face, and begs: "Lord, if you will, you can make me clean" (v.12). Jesus stretches out his hand and touches him: "I will; be clean." Immediately the leprosy leaves him (v.13). Jesus charges him to tell no one but to go and show himself to the priest and offer what Moses commanded, "for a proof to them" (v.14). Reports about Jesus spread further; great crowds gather to hear him and to be healed. But he would withdraw to desolate places and pray (vv.15–16). The two-fold conclusion — crowds gathering and Jesus withdrawing to pray — establishes the rhythm of ministry and solitude that characterizes Luke's portrait of Jesus.
12.historical_context: Leprosy in the OT (tsara'at in Hebrew, lepra in Greek) covered a range of skin diseases, not only modern Hansen's disease. Leviticus 13–14 prescribed detailed examination by the priest, quarantine procedures, and an elaborate purification ritual for those declared clean. The social isolation of the person with leprosy was total: exclusion from community, family, temple, and city (Leviticus 13:45–46). Jesus's touch is therefore doubly significant: by Levitical standards, touching a person with leprosy made one ritually unclean — but Jesus touches and the uncleanness flows from the leper to nowhere; instead, cleanness flows from Jesus to the leper. The instruction to show himself to the priest for the Mosaic cleansing rite (Lev 14) provides official verification and places Jesus's healing within the Torah's framework.
12.literary_structure: "Lord, if you will, you can make me clean" — the petition places the question of healing entirely in Jesus's will rather than his power. The man has no doubt about the power; he is uncertain about the will. Jesus's response closes that question immediately: "I will" (thelō). The certainty of divine power over leprosy is matched by the certainty of divine will toward this particular man. Luke's characteristically brief prayer note at v.16 — "but he would withdraw (hupechrōn, an imperfect: habitually would withdraw) to desolate places and pray" — contrasts the public fame growing at v.15 with the private spiritual discipline sustaining it. Fame and prayer are in inverse proportion in Luke: the more the fame, the more the withdrawal.
12.theological_application: The healing of leprosy represents one of the most radical acts of boundary-crossing in Jesus's ministry: leprosy was the quintessential marker of exclusion from community and worship. Jesus's willingness to touch the untouchable embodies the kingdom's arrival: the barriers that sin and its consequences erect between human beings and God's community are reversed. The instruction to go through the Mosaic purification rite is not merely respectful compliance with law but a theological statement: the healing that Jesus performs is the healing that the law always pointed toward and could never achieve (the leper's eventual return to community required the priest's declaration, not just personal wellness).
12.christology: That Jesus extends his hand and touches the man covered with leprosy before speaking the healing word underscores the incarnational logic of his ministry: he does not heal from a safe distance but enters the condition of the excluded person in order to restore them. The touch precedes the word — embodied compassion before verbal authority. The transmission of cleanness from Jesus to the leper rather than uncleanness from the leper to Jesus is the signature of what the incarnate Son does: he takes on what is ours and gives back what is his. Paul will give this its theological formulation in 2 Corinthians 5:21: "he made him who knew no sin to be sin for us, so that in him we might become the righteousness of God."

17.pericope_label: Jesus Forgives and Heals a Paralyzed Man
17.synthesis: Pharisees and teachers of the law from every village of Galilee, Judea, and Jerusalem are sitting there as Jesus is teaching. Men bring a paralyzed man on a bed, seeking to bring him in front of Jesus; finding no way because of the crowd, they go up on the roof and lower him through the tiles into the midst before Jesus (vv.17–19). Seeing their faith, Jesus says: "Man, your sins are forgiven you" (v.20). The scribes and Pharisees reason: "Who is this who speaks blasphemies? Who can forgive sins but God alone?" (v.21). Jesus perceives their thoughts: "Which is easier, to say 'Your sins are forgiven,' or to say 'Rise and walk'? But that you may know that the Son of Man has authority on earth to forgive sins" — he tells the paralyzed man to rise, take up his bed, and go home. He does it immediately, glorifying God (vv.22–25). Everyone is amazed: "We have seen extraordinary things today" (v.26).
17.historical_context: The presence of "Pharisees and teachers of the law from every village of Galilee, Judea, and Jerusalem" (v.17) signals that official scrutiny of Jesus has now begun in earnest. The roof-access in Palestinian houses was via an exterior staircase; tile roofs (Luke's version, vs. Mark's more primitive beaten-earth roof that can be "dug through") were common in wealthier Hellenistic construction. The theological controversy is precise: the scribal objection is not to the claim that God can forgive sins but to the implication that this human teacher can declare it as his own prerogative. In Jewish theology, divine forgiveness was mediated through the sacrificial system and the Day of Atonement; a rabbi pronouncing direct personal forgiveness was stepping outside all established mediation. Jesus's counter-question ("which is easier") is an argumentum a fortiori: the visible physical healing is the evidence that the invisible spiritual act (forgiveness) has also occurred.
17.literary_structure: The healing is bracketed by faith and glorification: "seeing their faith" (v.20) opens the healing, and "glorifying God" (v.25) closes it. The controversy-within-the-healing structure is uniquely productive: the healing serves as the proof of the forgiveness claim, making the physical miracle a christological argument. The "Son of Man has authority on earth to forgive sins" (v.24) is the first use of this title in Luke — introduced precisely in a controversy context, making the authority claim explicit. The crowd's conclusion, "We have seen extraordinary things today" (v.26), uses the word paradoxa — paradoxical, beyond expectation: what has happened does not fit any available category.
17.theological_application: The friends' persistence — carrying a man to a roof, making a hole, lowering him through it — is the paradigm of intercession: their faith is the vehicle of another's healing. "Seeing their faith" (v.20): the faith of the community acts on behalf of the one who cannot act for himself. This pictures both corporate prayer (the community bearing one another's burdens before the Lord) and baptism (carrying the helpless into the presence of Christ through the community's act). Jesus's first word to the paralyzed man is not about his body but about his soul: the forgiveness of sins is the deeper healing that the physical restoration signs. The sequence — forgiveness spoken first, healing following — establishes the order of priorities in Jesus's ministry.
17.christology: "The Son of Man has authority on earth to forgive sins" (v.24). This is the explicit christological claim the healing demonstrates: the title "Son of Man" — drawn from Daniel 7:13, the figure who receives universal authority from the Ancient of Days — is here filled with the content of forgiving authority that belongs to God alone. The scribes are correct that only God can forgive sins — and Jesus does not dispute this. He claims to exercise that divine prerogative as the Son of Man. The healing of the body is the credential for the forgiveness of the soul: the one who can command the paralyzed to walk can also command guilt to depart. Both are acts of the same divine authority exercised in human flesh.

27.pericope_label: Calling Levi and Eating with Sinners
27.synthesis: Jesus sees a tax collector named Levi sitting at his tax booth and says to him, "Follow me." Levi leaves everything and follows (vv.27–28). Levi gives a great feast in his house for Jesus, with a large company of tax collectors and others. The Pharisees and scribes grumble: "Why do you eat and drink with tax collectors and sinners?" (vv.29–30). Jesus: "Those who are well have no need of a physician, but those who are sick. I have not come to call the righteous but sinners to repentance" (vv.31–32). The disciples of John and the Pharisees fast while Jesus's disciples eat and drink; Jesus defends this: you cannot fast while the bridegroom is with you. When the bridegroom is taken away, then they will fast (vv.33–35). New wine requires new wineskins; no one drinks old wine and immediately desires new (vv.36–39).
27.historical_context: Tax collectors (telōnai) were typically Jews contracted to collect tolls on goods passing through their region, working for Roman revenue farmers; they were despised both for collaboration with Rome and for their reputation for overcharging. Levi/Matthew's tax booth was likely on the road from the Golan to Capernaum collecting transit duties. The Pharisaic practice of table fellowship was governed by strict purity rules: eating with those who were ritually unclean (tax collectors, sinners defined as habitual violators of Torah) rendered the observant Pharisee ritually impure. Fasting was practiced by Pharisees on Mondays and Thursdays in addition to the required Yom Kippur fast; John's disciples apparently maintained a rigorous ascetic practice as well. The new wine / old wineskins metaphor (vv.37–38) reflects actual Palestinian practice: new wine undergoing fermentation expands and would burst dried, inelastic old wineskins.
27.literary_structure: The Levi call-and-feast is the first of three escalating controversies in Luke 5–6: eating with sinners (ch. 5) → fasting dispute (ch. 5) → Sabbath controversies (ch. 6). Each controversy probes a different dimension of the religious boundaries Jesus crosses. The physician metaphor (vv.31–32) inverts the Pharisaic logic: the Pharisees avoid the sick to stay well; the physician goes to the sick precisely because they are sick. Luke's unique addition in v.39 — "no one after drinking old wine desires new, for he says 'The old is good'" — is an ironic explanation of why the Pharisees resist Jesus's new-wine kingdom: they have become accustomed to what they have and do not perceive what they are missing.
27.theological_application: "I have not come to call the righteous but sinners to repentance" (v.32). The table fellowship that scandalized the Pharisees is Jesus's enacted proclamation of who the kingdom is for. The feast in Levi's house — where tax collectors and sinners eat with the one who will shortly eat the Last Supper with his disciples — is a foretaste of the messianic banquet, the great end-time feast of the kingdom. Repentance (unique addition in Luke's version, v.32) is the human response that the feast requires: the sinners who come to the table are not endorsed in their sin but called to a turning. The table is both welcome and summons.
27.christology: The bridegroom metaphor for Jesus (vv.34–35) draws on the OT's portrayal of YHWH as the husband of Israel (Isaiah 54:5; Hosea 2:16; Ezekiel 16) and anticipates the NT's use of the marriage metaphor for Christ and the church (Ephesians 5:25–32; Revelation 19:7). The days of Jesus's presence are wedding days — the time of celebration and feasting, not fasting. "When the bridegroom is taken away" (v.35) is the first hint of the Passion in Luke's narrative: the joy of the present is bounded by a coming separation, and fasting will resume when the bridegroom is absent. The entire feast of Jesus's ministry is eschatological: it begins the wedding banquet that the Passion will interrupt and the resurrection will resume.
